era_context: The late 17th century: the Ottoman Empire faces increasing pressure from European powers; the Qing Dynasty consolidates control over China; Louis XIV's France dominates European politics; the Scientific Revolution accelerates; global maritime trade networks expand under European colonial powers; indigenous peoples in the Americas resist European colonization.

events.title: Pueblo Revolt in New Mexico
events.description: The Pueblo Revolt, led by Po'pay, a Tewa religious leader, erupted in August 1680 in New Mexico. The revolt united multiple Pueblo nations against Spanish colonial rule, resulting in the expulsion of Spanish colonists from the region. The uprising was motivated by Spanish suppression of indigenous religious practices, forced labor, and cultural oppression. The Pueblo Revolt was one of the most successful indigenous uprisings against European colonization in North America, and it resulted in the establishment of an independent Pueblo confederacy that lasted until the Spanish reconquest in 1692.

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and military reports
Pueblo oral traditions and later accounts
Historiographic consensus on the Pueblo Revolt

events.certainty_note: The Pueblo Revolt is well-documented in Spanish colonial records and confirmed by Pueblo oral traditions; the date of August 1680 is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Three Feudatories War continues in Qing China
events.description: The Three Feudatories War, which had begun in 1673, continued in 1680 with the Kangxi Emperor's forces gradually gaining the upper hand against the rebel generals Wu Sangui, Shang Kexi, and Geng Jingmao. The year 1680 saw continued military operations and the consolidation of Qing control over southern China. The war would be concluded in 1681 with the final suppression of the rebellion.

sources.name: Qing official records and imperial chronicles
Historiographic consensus on the Three Feudatories War

events.certainty_note: The Three Feudatories War is well-documented in Qing records; 1680 falls within the final phase of the conflict.

events.title: Louis XIV's cultural dominance; Versailles construction continues
events.description: In 1680, the construction of the Palace of Versailles continued under the direction of Jules Hardouin-Mansart. The palace was becoming the symbol of Louis XIV's absolute power and cultural dominance in Europe. The year 1680 saw the continuation of the artistic and architectural projects that would make Versailles the model for European absolutism. The court was still based in Paris, but the move to Versailles would occur in 1682.

sources.name: French court records and architectural records
Memoirs of courtiers and contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: The construction of Versailles is well-documented in French records; 1680 falls within the period of active construction.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Cretan War) concludes
events.description: The Ottoman-Venetian War, also known as the Cretan War, which had lasted from 1645 to 1669, had already concluded with the Ottoman conquest of Crete. However, tensions between the Ottoman Empire and Venice continued in 1680, with ongoing naval conflicts and diplomatic negotiations. The year 1680 saw the Treaty of Constantinople being negotiated, which would formally end hostilities between the two powers.

sources.name: Ottoman diplomatic records and the Topkapi Palace archives
Venetian diplomatic records and state archives

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Ottoman-Venetian negotiations in 1680 is well-documented, though the precise dating of the Treaty of Constantinople varies slightly across sources.

events.title: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns; Mughal expansion continues
events.description: Aurangzeb continued his military campaigns in the Deccan in 1680, pursuing aggressive expansion and the suppression of regional powers. The Mughal Empire was at or near its territorial zenith during this period. The year 1680 saw ongoing military operations and the consolidation of Mughal control over newly conquered territories. These campaigns were costly and contributed to the strain on the Mughal imperial system.

sources.name: Mughal court records and imperial chronicles

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Mughal military campaigns in 1680 is well-documented, though specific battle dates vary across sources.

events.title: English colonial consolidation in North America
events.description: English colonial governance in North America continued to consolidate in 1680. The year saw the establishment of the Dominion of New England (formally established in 1686, but preparations began in the early 1680s), which represented an attempt by the Crown to centralize control over the American colonies. This period also saw increased English-French colonial competition in North America.

sources.name: English Crown records and colonial charters
Historiographic consensus on colonial American history

events.certainty_note: The general trend of royal consolidation is well-documented; specific events in 1680 are less precisely dated than the formal establishment of the Dominion in 1686.

disconfirming_evidence: The Pueblo Revolt is sometimes portrayed as a complete and permanent expulsion of Spanish colonists; however, modern scholarship emphasizes that the Spanish reconquest in 1692 was relatively swift, and that the Pueblo confederacy was not as unified as sometimes depicted. Additionally, some accounts overstate the role of Po'pay as a unified leader; recent scholarship notes the diversity of Pueblo leadership and motivations. The Three Feudatories War is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory for the Kangxi Emperor; however, modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of the campaign and the role of negotiation. The Ottoman-Venetian tensions are sometimes overstated; by 1680, both powers were exhausted and seeking peace.
historiographic_note: The year 1680 is well-documented in Spanish, Chinese, French, Ottoman, and English sources. The Pueblo Revolt is particularly well-documented in Spanish colonial records, though indigenous perspectives are less well-represented. The Three Feudatories War is extensively documented in Qing records. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are underrepresented. Indigenous perspectives from North America are sparse or absent from European records. The Mughal sources are court-centric and may underrepresent regional resistance and dissent. Bias toward written records means oral traditions and archaeological evidence are underutilized.

graph_edges.note: The Pueblo Revolt in 1680 prompted Spanish reconquest preparations in 1681.
The Pueblo Revolt in 1680 was followed by the Spanish reconquest of New Mexico in 1692.
The continuation of the Three Feudatories War in 1680 led to its suppression in 1681.
The construction of Versailles in 1680 culminated in Louis XIV's move to the palace in 1682.
Ottoman-Venetian tensions in 1680 occurred during the same period as Ottoman preparations for the Siege of Vienna in 1683.
1680 falls within the final decades of Aurangzeb's reign, a period of imperial overextension.
